original_abstract: Fires represent a significant risk to the safety and infrastructure of modern smart cities. While early detection is critical to mitigating property damage and saving lives, traditional sensor-based systems often struggle with latency, limited coverage, and high false-alarm rates in complex urban environments. This paper introduces the Smart Fire Detection System (SFDS), an advanced real-time approach built upon the state-of-the-art YOLOv8 deep learning algorithm. The SFDS is integrated into a multi-layered smart city framework consisting of IoT, Fog, Cloud, and Application layers, designed to optimize data processing speeds and response times. Unlike existing models, the proposed system was trained on a comprehensive dataset of 26,520 images encompassing a diverse array of indoor and outdoor fire and smoke scenarios under varying lighting and noise conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the SFDS achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching a high precision rate of 97.1% across all classes and a recall rate of 99.3% for smoke detection. By leveraging the speed and accuracy of YOLOv8, the system significantly reduces false alerts and provides a cost-effective, scalable solution for public safety, forest monitoring, and intelligent security infrastructures.
humanized_abstract: Fire accidents pose a significant threat to the infrastructure of the smart cities. Early fire detection helps in saving the valuable lives and preventing the damage to properties. The existing sensor based fire detection systems are ineffective due to the delayed response, limited coverage area, frequent false alarms etc. In this paper, an efficient real time fire detection system namely Smart Fire Detection System (SFDS) is proposed using the state of the art YOLOv8 algorithm for detection of fire. The proposed system is embedded into a multi layered smart city architecture consisting of IoT layer, Fog layer, Cloud layer and Application layer in order to achieve real time processing. The existing systems generally consider few classes of fire and smoke. However, in this paper, the proposed system is evaluated for a large dataset of 26520 images comprising of various classes of fire and smoke scenarios for different ambient conditions such as light and noise. The SFDS achieves an average precision of 97.1 % and an average recall of 99.3 % for the class of smoke. The proposed YOLOv8 based system is efficient in terms of reduction of false alarms. The proposed system provides a cost effective solution for public safety, forest monitoring and intelligent security systems.
